one_liner: A 22-year-old college student is brought to clinic after his roommate witnessed him fall, stiffen, and jerk his arms and legs rhythmically for about 90 seconds, with loss of consciousness and tongue biting. This is his third such episode in six months.

stage2.hpi_extension: The roommate describes three episodes over the past 6 months. In each, the patient suddenly went stiff (tonic phase), then had rhythmic jerking of all four extremities (clonic phase) for 60-90 seconds, was incontinent of urine during the last one, and was confused for about 20 minutes afterward (postictal state). He reports biting his tongue laterally. He has never had a head injury, CNS infection, or prior neurologic disease. No recent fever, no illicit drugs, no alcohol withdrawal. No prodromal aura. Episodes have occurred during the day while studying.
stage2.family_history: Paternal uncle with 'a seizure disorder' since childhood
stage2.social_history: College junior. Occasional alcohol (<3 drinks/week, none in 48 hours). No illicit drugs. Sleeps 5 hours/night during exams.
stage2.physical_exam: Alert and oriented. Lateral tongue laceration healing. Neuro: cranial nerves II-XII intact, strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes 2+ and symmetric, no Babinski, gait normal, no cerebellar signs. No meningismus. Cardiovascular, pulmonary, and abdominal exams normal.

lab_results.result: EEG with sleep deprivation and photic stimulation: generalized 4-5 Hz spike-and-wave discharges activated by hyperventilation and photic driving; background is otherwise normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Generalized spike-and-wave discharges on EEG in a young adult with tonic-clonic seizures support the diagnosis of a generalized epilepsy, most commonly juvenile myoclonic epilepsy or primary generalized tonic-clonic epilepsy.

lab_results.result: MRI brain (epilepsy protocol with thin-cut hippocampal sequences): no structural lesion, no mesial temporal sclerosis, no cortical malformation, no tumor.
lab_results.clinical_significance: MRI excludes structural (symptomatic) causes such as tumor, cortical dysplasia, mesial temporal sclerosis, or stroke. Normal MRI is consistent with idiopathic (genetic) generalized epilepsy.

lab_results.result: Sodium 139, glucose 94, calcium 9.4, magnesium 2.0, creatinine 0.8, LFTs normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Excludes hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, and uremia as provocative causes of seizures.

stage5.final_clue: He is started on levetiracetam, counseled about seizure triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol, flashing lights), and told he cannot drive until he has been seizure-free per state requirements. A follow-up EEG in 6 months is planned.

debrief.classic_presentation: Epilepsy is defined as two or more unprovoked seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with a high recurrence risk. Seizures are classified as focal (partial) — arising from one hemisphere, with or without impaired awareness and with or without secondary generalization — or generalized, which includes tonic-clonic (grand mal), absence (petit mal; brief staring spells with 3 Hz spike-and-wave), myoclonic, atonic (drop attacks), and tonic types. Classic clues to a true generalized tonic-clonic seizure include lateral tongue biting, incontinence, a postictal confusional period, and postictal elevation of CK and prolactin.
debrief.pathophysiology: A seizure is an episode of abnormal synchronous neuronal firing; epilepsy is the disease state of recurrent unprovoked seizures. Excess excitation (glutamate/NMDA) or deficient inhibition (GABA) tips neuronal circuits into hypersynchronous discharge. Causes are age-dependent: genetic/idiopathic generalized epilepsies and developmental malformations in children and young adults; mesial temporal sclerosis (after childhood febrile seizures), tumors, and post-traumatic scars in adults; and stroke in the elderly. Focal seizures typically arise from structural lesions, while generalized epilepsies are often genetic.
debrief.treatment_overview: Anti-epileptic drug (AED) choice depends on seizure type. Broad-spectrum agents (focal + generalized): valproate, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, topiramate, zonisamide. Focal seizures: carbamazepine (first-line, but Stevens-Johnson risk especially in HLA-B*1502 Asian patients), oxcarbazepine, phenytoin. Absence seizures: ethosuximide (first-line). Teratogens to avoid in pregnancy: valproate (neural tube defects, lowest IQ in offspring), phenytoin (fetal hydantoin syndrome), carbamazepine (neural tube defects). Women of childbearing age should take folate and ideally be switched to lamotrigine or levetiracetam. Driving is restricted until seizure-free per state law.
high_yield_pearls: Epilepsy = 2 or more unprovoked seizures >24 h apart, or 1 seizure with high recurrence risk.
Ethosuximide is first-line for absence seizures (3 Hz spike-and-wave on EEG).
Carbamazepine is first-line for focal seizures — watch for Stevens-Johnson syndrome (HLA-B*1502), aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis, and SIADH.
Valproate is broad-spectrum but teratogenic (neural tube defects) and causes hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, and weight gain.
Lamotrigine is broad-spectrum and preferred in pregnancy, but titrate slowly to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Seizures — Epilepsy' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Epilepsy drugs' table.
